turns.content: I'm trying to enforce a hard memory limit on my Rust binary in the nix environment, but I can't seem to get it working. Here is what I have: let ulimit = libc::RLIMIT_AS; std::fs::write("/proc/sys/fs/nodma", "1").unwrap(); unsafe { libc::setrlimit(ulimit, &mut limit) }; println!("Limit set");

The code you posted attempts to write a file and then call setrlimit directly on the raw libc constant. In Rust it is better to use the crate nix::fcntl to wrap these calls safely rather than using unsafe blocks with raw pointers. You should define an nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit struct that holds the limit value, then pass that to nix::fcntl::setrlimit instead of writing to /proc directly.
I tried using nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit but it says I need to implement a trait or use a different struct. Let me look at the actual type definition in the crate and see if that matches what I'm doing.

The Rlimit type in nix is actually nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit which wraps a libc::rlimit structure. You do not need to implement any trait for it because the crate already provides conversion methods from Rust structs to the c types required by setrlimit. Just create an instance of that specific type and pass it along with the resource identifier like RLIMIT_AS or RLIMIT_FDATA.
Wait, I thought I had to manually map the values and convert them myself because the documentation didn't show an easy way. That sounds too complicated for a simple limit setting.

The crate handles all that mapping internally so you don't need to do any manual conversion or custom traits. You simply pass the nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit instance containing your desired limit and the appropriate resource identifier like RLIMIT_AS directly into setrlimit without any extra steps.
I'm getting an error now saying that I cannot construct that type correctly. It seems like the fields are not what I expected or maybe there's a missing dependency in my Cargo.toml.

The nix crate must be added to your dependencies with exact version constraints before you can use those types without compilation errors. Ensure that nix = "0.27" or higher is specified in your Cargo.toml file so the Rlimit struct matches the current API.
That fixed it, but now I want to verify that this actually prevents memory growth at runtime like setrlimit should. Can you explain how nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit interacts with the kernel once set?

Once you call setrlimit with an nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit instance, the kernel enforces the soft and hard limits immediately for that process. Any attempt to allocate more than the specified soft limit will fail with SIGKILL or SIGSEGV depending on the exact configuration, which is why this approach is effective for enforcing caps.
